summary:
Claim: Chewing stroke begins at maximum intercuspation under normal conditions.  

Evidence: - Maximum intercuspation represents full tooth contact and serves as the biomechanically stable baseline for mastication. - It is universally accepted in dental anatomy as the origin of all mandibular movements prior to excursions.  

Answer: C  

Despite orthodontic-induced changes in occlusal relationships, maximum intercuspation remains the anatomical and functional anchor for chewing initiation. While some clinical contexts—such as TMD—may favor centric relation (CR) for therapeutic reasons, chewing stroke itself originates from maximal tooth contact, not from functional joint stability alone. Thus, even when occlusal planes are altered post-treatment, the fundamental biomechanical origin of chewing motion persists at maximum intercuspation. The distinction between CR and CO lies in clinical application, not in physiological onset of masticatory movement. Therefore, the correct choice reflecting established anatomical principles is C.

While some clinical contexts—such as TMD—may favor centric relation (CR) for therapeutic reasons, chewing stroke itself originates from maximal tooth contact, not from functional joint stability alone. Answer choice: D
